coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots boost the electrochemical supercapacitive performance and cyclic stability of MoS2
coredata.dc:creator: El Sharkawy, Heba M.
Dhmees, Abdelghaffar S.
Tamman, A.R.
El Sabagh, S.M.
Aboushahba, R.M.
Allam, Nageh K.
coredata.dc:description: We report on the successful synthesis of flower-like MoS2/N-doped carbon dots (CDs) composite nanospheres via a facile solvothermal method making use of the layered structure of MoS2 and the excellent conductivity of CDs. The structural, compositional, and morphological properties of the fabricated electrodes were investigated using XRD, XPS, EDS, Raman spectroscopy, FTIR, TEM, and FE-SEM techniques. The addition of carbon dots to MoS2 improved the cyclic stability, wettability, and conductivity of the fabricated composites upon their use as supercapacitor electrodes. The supercapacitor electrodes showed a specific capacitance as high as 250.55 Fg−1 at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and 149.21 Fg−1 at 0.5 A/g. A symmetric supercapacitor device composed of MoS2/NCDs composite and activated carbon as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively delivered an energy density and a power density of 20.01 Wh/kg and 350 W/kg, respectively with 98% Coulombic efficiency after 5000 cycles, indicating the high performance of the fabricated electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2
N-doped carbon dots
Supercapacitor
Energy density
Power density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microwave synthesis of n-doped carbon dots (CDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (a) NCDs, (b) MoS2/NCDs composite, (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of MoS2/NCDs composite. The insets in (a) and (b) show the contact angle measurements for MoS2 and MoS2/NCDs composite, respectively.
(a) EDX spectra and elemental mapping of the MoS2/NCDs composite.
(a,b) XRD pattern of CNDs and MoS2/NCDs composite, respectively, (c) Raman spectrum of CNDs, (d) FTIR spectrum of NCDs, and (e) FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized MoS2/NCDs composite.
(a) XPS survey and high resolution XPS scans of (b) Mo 3d, (c) S 2p, (d) C 1 s, and (e) N 1 s for the as-synthesized MoS2/NCDs composite.

(a) CV curves of bare MoS2 electrodes at different scan rates, (b) CV curves of the MoS2/NCDs electrodes at different scan rates, (c) constant current charge/discharge (GCD) profiles of bare MoS2 at various current densities, (d) constant current charge/discharge (GCD) profiles of the MoS2/CDs electrodes, (e) the calculated Csp versus discharge current densities of the MoS2/CDs electrodes, and (f) Tafel plot for the MoS2/NCDs electrodes.

Electrochemical characterization of the MoS2/NCDs //AC device: (a) CV curves at various scan rates, (b) GCD curve at different current densities, (c) cycling stability and columbic efficiency over 5000 GCD cycles, (d) Ragone plot of the asymmetric device, (e) Nyquist plots of the asymmetric device, (f) glow ELD using two devices connected in series.
Comparison of the specific capacitance, cyclic stability, power density and energy density of the reported supercapacitor electrodes made of MoS2 and/or MoS2-carbon composites to our work.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots boost the electrochemical supercapacitive performance and cyclic stability of MoS2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Materials Laboratory, School of Sciences and Engineering, The American University in Cairo, 11835, New Cairo, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Energy Materials Laboratory, School of Sciences and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aEnergy Materials Laboratory, School of Sciences and Engineering, The American University in Cairo, 11835, New Cairo, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analysis and Evaluation, Egyptian Petroleum Research Institute, 11727, Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Analysis and Evaluation, Egyptian Petroleum Research Institute, 11727, Cairo, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Chemistry Department, Al-Azhar University, Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cFaculty of Science, Chemistry Department, Al-Azhar University, Cairo, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on the successful synthesis of flower-like MoS2/N-doped carbon dots (CDs) composite nanospheres via a facile solvothermal method making use of the layered structure of MoS2 and the excellent conductivity of CDs. The structural, compositional, and morphological properties of the fabricated electrodes were investigated using XRD, XPS, EDS, Raman spectroscopy, FTIR, TEM, and FE-SEM techniques. The addition of carbon dots to MoS2 improved the cyclic stability, wettability, and conductivity of the fabricated composites upon their use as supercapacitor electrodes. The supercapacitor electrodes showed a specific capacitance as high as 250.55 Fg−1 at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and 149.21 Fg−1 at 0.5 A/g. A symmetric supercapacitor device composed of MoS2/NCDs composite and activated carbon as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively delivered an energy density and a power density of 20.01 Wh/kg and 350 W/kg, respectively with 98% Coulombic efficiency after 5000 cycles, indicating the high performance of the fabricated electrodes.

ce:section.ce:para: A great deal of attention has recently been devoted to environmentally friendly and low-cost energy conversion and storage devices [1,2] due to the depletion of fossil fuel reservoirs, increasing crude oil prices, and the environmental pollution including global warming and climate change. Among the various electrochemical energy storage systems, supercapacitors have a plethora of unique electrochemical properties including safe operation, long cycle life, rapid charge/discharge, higher power density than batteries, and higher specific capacitance than conventional capacitors [3–5]. However, the low energy density of supercapacitors is the bottle neck limiting their wide use in large-scale applications [6]. The most commercial supercapacitors are electric double layer capacitors (carbon-based material) with limited energy density as low as 10 W h kg−1. In this regard, asymmetric supercapacitors composed of a pseudocapacitive material and carbon material are proposed as alternative devices with improved performance. Pseudocapacitive materials include transition metal oxides, nitrides, and sulphides, which can deliver higher energy density than the double layer carbon materials [7,8]. Among the various transition metal sulfides investigated, layered transition metal dichalcogenides (TMD), such as MoS2, have triggered intensive interest in energy conversion and storage applications due to their unique chemical, physical, electronic, and optical properties as well as their layered structure [9]. MoS2 possesses a typical layered structure similar to graphene, composed of three layers where a Mo layer is positioned in between two S layers [10]. MoS2 is widely used in many industrial applications such as catalysis, water splitting, electrochemical devices, lubricants, hydrogen storage, capacitors, and hydrodesulphurization [11]. Among those, there are only few reports on MoS2 as a supercapacitor electrode material. Soon and Loh tested MoS2 as an electrode material for supercapacitor and reported an electric double layer behavior owing to the large surface area [12]. Huang et al. reported the hydrothermal preparation of MoS2 and their use as supercapacitor electrode material, which exhibited a specific capacitance of 98 Fg−1 [13]. Krishnamoorthy et al. showed that sphere-like MoS2 nanomaterials revealed a capacitance of 106 Fg−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV/s [14]. A mesoporous MoS2 with flower-like morphology showed a capacitance of 19.1 Fg−1 [15]. A capacitance of 89.02 Fg−1 at 5 mVs−1 was reported for MoS2 nanosheet prepared by ball milling mechanical exfoliation [16]. However, MoS2 exhibits poor electronic conductivity and poor rate performance, which limit its practical applications [17]. To overcome these shortcomings, many efforts have been focused on combining carbon-based materials carbon with MoS2 to increase the conductivity, the surface wettability, and surface area of MoS2 [18]. Carbon dots (CDs) are novel 0D carbon nanoparticles, which have been utilized in a plethora of applications such as photocatalysis, photovoltaic devices, sensors, and bioimaging [19]. Recently, CDs have attracted much attention as a potential class of materials for supercapacitor applications due to their ease of production compared to graphene and carbon nanotubes, low cost, more flexible for constructing various structures than multidimensional carbon materials, good hydrophilicity, ability to be doped with hetero atoms such as N and O, and stability. The use of CDs in composite with other materials has provided more pathways and interspaces for both ions transfer and volume change and acted as good conductive agent to increase the conductivity and electrochemical performance [8,20,21].

Based on the above merits of MoS2 and CDs, we aimed at the fabrication of MoS2/NCDs composites and test their performance as electrodes in supercapacitor devices. While MoS2 was synthesized via a simple solvothermal method, a facile microwave route was used to synthesize N-doped CDs. Then, the MoS2/NCDs nanocomposites were fabricated in one step via solvothermal method. Upon the use of the MoS2/NCDs composite as the positive electrode and a negative electrode made of activated carbon, the supercapacitor device exhibited a high energy density of 20.01 Wh/kg with very high capacity retention rate after 5000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave synthesis of n-doped carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Urea (3 g) and citric acid (3 g) have been added to deionized water (10 mL), forming a clear solution. Then, the solution was heated in a microwave oven (750 W) for 5 min, where the solution changed from colorless to dark-brown clustered solid, indicating the formation of CDs. To remove the residual small molecules, this solid was transferred to a vacuum oven and heated at 60 °C for 1 h. To remove large or agglomerated particles, an aqueous solution of the CDs was centrifuged (3000 rpm, 20 min). Finally, the resulting brown aqueous solution was put in rotary evaporator at 80 °C to obtain solid CDs.

3 mmol of sodium molybdate (0.726 g) was dissolved in 40 ml ethylene glycol solution and then sonicated for 30 min at room temperature. Followed by addition of the 15 mmol of thiourea (1.1418 g). Purified CDs after dialysis treatment (0.05 g) were added and sonicated for another 30 min. The solvothermal reaction was carried out in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. Then, the resulted black powder was collected and thoroughly washed with water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, vacuum drying of the sample was carried out at 70 °C for 12 h.

Crystal structure and composition of the prepared nanostructured materials were analyzed by means of x-ray diffraction using a PANalytical X'pert PRO diffractometer (with Cu Kα radiation), EDS associated with FESEM, and XPS (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo-Scientific). A Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer was used to collect the FT-IR spectra using KBr pellets. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Zeiss SEM Ultra 60, 5 kV) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JOEL JEM-2100) operating at 200 kV accelerating voltage were used to image the fabricated composites. A Quantachrome NOVA Station A (version 11.03) was employed for the N2 gas adsorption/desorption measurements.

The electrochemical performance of the prepared materials was measured via a BioLogic SP- 300 potentiostat. The three-electrode system consists of a calomel reference electrode, Pt coil counter electrode, and the composite working electrode in 6.0 M KOH. The working electrode was made as a slurry of 70% MoS2/NCDs composite, 10% polyvinylidene fluoride, and 20% carbon black in dimethyl formamide on (1 × 1 cm2) graphite sheet substrate. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out over a potential window from 0 to 0.6 V with scan rates ranging from 10 to 100 mV s−1. Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) experiments were performed at 0.5 - 10 A/g and the EIS was carried out over a wide range of frequency (10 kHz–0.01 Hz) with a sinusoidal perturbation of 10 mV amplitude and the steady-state open circuit potential.

The asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled using activated carbon (AC) and (MoS2/NCDs composite) as the negative and positive electrodes, respectively, a filter paper as the separator in 6.0 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. The AC electrode was prepared as a slurry of 90% AC + 10% PVDF in DMF on graphite sheet. The stored charge (Q) was balanced by balancing the loaded mass ratios of the active materials on each electrode (m) in a voltage range of ΔV according to Eqs. (1) and (2) below:
(1)
Q + = Q −
(2)
m +
m −
=
c −
Δ
V
c +
Δ
V

Fig. 1 a depicts an FESEM image of the as-prepared bare MoS2, revealing the size dispersion of the particles, which was relatively small with the majority of the particles in the range of 15.8 to 26.7 nm in diameter [22]. Fig. 1b shows an FESEM image of the as-prepared MoS2/NCDs composite, revealing the formation of flower-like structure that has dense stretching petal-like edges, indicating the ultrathin nature of the MoS2 nanosheets as confirmed via the TEM image shown in Fig. 1c,d. The HRTEM image reveals MoS2 nanosheets composed of few layers with an interlayer spacing of 1.38 nm, which can be attributed to the incorporation of NCDs into the MoS2 nanosheets. Note that the enlarged interlayer spacing is beneficial for ions intercalation that would improve the electrochemical performance of the composite. The insets in Fig. 1a,b depict the contact angel of bare MoS2 and the MoS2/NCDs composite, indicating the improvement of the hydrophilicity of the composite (79°) over that of pure MoS2 (90°). The EDS spectrum and elemental mapping of the as-fabricated MoS2/NCDs composite, Fig. 2 , indicate the coexistence of Mo, S, N, and C with no extra peaks of foreign elements, revealing the high purity of the synthesized MoS2/NCDs composite [23].

Fig. 3 a depicts the x-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the as-prepared CDs, indicating a broad diffraction peak at ca. 27°, indicating a highly disordered structure of the fabricated CDs [24]. Fig. 3b shows the XRD pattern of the as-prepared composite of MoS2/NCDs. The peaks at 14.1°, 32.9°, and 58.6° correspond to the (002), (100), and (110) crystal planes of MoS2 [25]. The high broadening of the peaks reveals the random orientation of the MoS2 layers [26]. The diffraction peak at 9.1° corresponds to CDs [27]. Fig. 3c illustrates the Raman spectra of the CDs, revealing two broad peaks at 1590 and 1380 cm−1, which are attributed to the G band (sp2-hybridized) and the D band (sp3-hybridized), respectively. The intensity ratio of the disordered D band to crystalline G band (ID/IG) of the prepared CDs is around 1.01, indicating the existence of a large number of defect sites and graphitic carbon [28]. The surface functional groups of the prepared CDs were identified via FT-IR spectroscopy as shown Fig. 3d. The main absorption band at 3417 cm−1 is related to ν(OH) and the absorption band at 3234 cm−1 is related to ν(NH). These functional groups can be held responsible for improving the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs in aqueous systems [29]. The weak absorption bands at 2920 and 2850 cm−1 are related to ν(C–H) and absorption bands from 1350 to 1460 cm−1 are assigned to δ(CH2). These indicate the presence of methyl groups on the CDs surface [30]. The absorption bands at 1714 and 1630 cm−1 are related to ν(CO) and amide bonding from a CO stretch, respectively [31]. The FTIR spectrum of the composite, Fig. 3e, showed an additional small peak at 450 cm−1, which is characteristic of Mo-S stretching vibration [22].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is an effective method to identify the composition and valence states of the elements in a material. Fig. 4
shows the XPS spectra for the as-synthesized MoS2/NCDs composite. The survey spectra, Fig. 4a, shows four peaks corresponding to Mo 3d, S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, indicating the successful formation of MoS2/CDs composite. The high resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d, Fig. 4b, reveal two peaks at 228.41 and 231.68 eV ascribed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively, suggesting the existence of the dominant 1T phase [32]. The high resolution S 2p XPS spectra, Fig. 4c, reveal the existence of beaks related to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 located at 161.35 and 162.5 eV indicating that S2− is the dominant oxidation state [33] The appearance of another S2p peak at 168.31 eV indicated surface adsorbed oxidized sulfur species [34]. The C 1 s spectra, Fig. 4d, reveal the presence of oxidized C-containing species (C–OH) at 286.61 eV besides the C–C peak at 284.49 eV [35]. The N 1 s spectra, Fig. 4e shows the N 1 s spectra, indicating two photoemission peaks at 396 and 398.7 eV that can be ascribed to the CN bonding and amine/amide groups, respectively, in agreement with the FTIR spectra. The NCDs is expecting to play a vital role in improving the surface wettability and pseudocapacitive behavior of the electrode material [36].

Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were used to evaluate the performance of the fabricated materials in an aqueous 6 M KOH electrolyte in a three-electrode electrochemical cell. The CV scans of the bare MoS2 electrodes at different scan rates over a potential window of 0 to 0.4 V are shown in Fig. 5
a. The CV curves have almost similar rectangular shapes without any battery-like redox peaks. This indicates a pseudocapacitive behavior of the material, where the K+ cation from the electrolyte would diffuse between the MoS2 layers and intercalate with them. Moreover, non-faradic process, where the charges accumulate at the electrode/electrolyte interface, may also occur [37].
(3)
Mo
S 2
+
K
+
+
e
−
→ MoS − S K
(
Faradic
charge
transfer
mechanism
)
(4)
Mo
S 2
+
K
+
+
e
−
→ Mo
S 2
− K
(
Non − Faradic
charge
transfer
mechanism
)

Fig. 5b depicts the CV curves of the MoS2/NCDs composite at different scan rates (5–100 mV/s) in the potential window from 0 to 0.4 V. Similar to the bare MoS2 electrodes, all CV curves retain similar rectangular shapes within the experimental scan rates. However, the obtained currents for the MoS2/NCDs composites are higher than those of the bare MoS2 counterpart electrodes and consequently higher specific capacitance (Cs) based on Eq. (5).
(5)
C s =
∫ I
d V
V m
Δ
V
where v is the potential scan rate, m is the mass of electrode material, I is the response current density, and ΔV is the potential window. The calculated specific capacitances at 10 mV/s were 77.23 F/g and 250.55 F/g for pure MoS2 and MoS2/NCDs composite, respectively, indicating the positive effect of CDs in boosting the capacitance of MoS2. This may be attributed to the enhanced conductivity of the composite upon the addition of CDs [38] as bare MoS2 suffers from low conductivity. Also, the introduction of CDs seems to provide fast electron transfer ability and reduces [39]. The production cost of CDs is lower than that of other carbon materials such as graphene and carbon nanotube derivatives [40]. All these merits add to the potential of the MoS2/NCDs composite as a promising electrode material for supercapacitor application.

The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) technique is an accurate method to determine the electrochemical supercapacitive behavior of electroactive materials. The chronopotentiometry (E vs. time) is the GCD output, where a constant specific current is applied on the electrode and the potential response is recorded versus time [41]. The charge/discharge plots for bare MoS2 and MoS2/NCDs electrodes at various current densities (0.5–10 A g−1) are shown in Fig. 5c and d. The nearly symmetric charge and discharge further validates the superior pseudocapacitance behavior of the electrode materials, in agreement with the CV results. The specific capacitance (Csp) can be calculated from the discharge curve according to Eq. (6).
(6)
C
s p
=
I
d t
m
d V
where I is the discharging current (A), dt is the discharging time (s), dV is the potential window (V), and m is the mass of the active material (g) within the electrode. The specific capacitance calculated from the discharge curve at current density 0.5 A/g was 30.66 F/g for MoS2 and increased to 149.21 F/g for the MoS2/NCDs composite. The enhanced specific capacitance of the composite material can be attributed to the high surface area of 139 m2/g (Fig. S1) and the presence of functional groups such as OH, amide, and COOH (Fig. 2), which increase the hydrophilicity of the MoS2/NCDs electrode (Fig. 1). The improved hydrophilicity would enhance the contact of the composite electrode with the electrolyte, ensuring fast transfer of charge carriers and ions during the charge–discharge processes [42,43]. The dependence of specific capacitance on current density is illustrated in Fig. 5e. Upon increasing the current density, the specific capacitance slightly decreases. Fig. 5f shows the Tafel plot (i= avb) for the MoS2/NCDs composite electrode, where the slope (b) is used to determine the predominant behavior of the electrode materials for energy storage. While a slope of b = 1 indicates a double layer behavior, a slope of b = 0.5 indicates diffusion-controlled behavior. The b value for the MoS2/CDs composite was found to be 0.740, indicating the predominance of pseudocapacitance behavior [44].

To further evaluate the as-prepared MoS2/NCDs composite electrodes for real applications, a two-electrode asymmetric supercapacitor device was assembled using the MoS2/NCDs composite as the positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as a negative electrode. The mass ratio of the positive electrode to the negative electrode is set in a suitable ratio for the charge balance. The CV curves of the asymmetric supercapacitor (MoS2/NCDs composite//AC) is shown in Fig. 6
a. The performance of the device was investigated in potential window 0–1.4 at different scan rates (5–100 mV/s), revealing a pseudocapacitive behavior with a quasi-rectangular CV shape. The shape of the CV curves remains almost unchanged upon increasing the scan rate, confirming the good reversibility and rate capability of the fabricated device with a good operating cell voltage. Fig. 6b illustrates the GCD curves of the fabricated device at different current densities (0.5 to 10 A g − 1) over a potential window of 0–1.4 V. The GCD curves generally revealed a nearly triangle shape with obvious symmetry of the curves, indicating excellent electrochemical reversibility with good Columbic efficiency. The maximum calculated specific capacitance of the asymmetric supercapacitor was 73.92 F/g at 0.5 A/ g. Fig. 6c shows the dependence of the Columbic efficiency (Ƞ) of the electrode material on the current density. Columbic efficiency (Ƞ) is the ratio of the discharging time (td ) to the charging time (tc ) and can be calculated from GCD graph using Eq. (7).
(7)
η % =
t d
t c
× 100

The Columbic efficiency (Ƞ) of the device increases with increasing current density, e.g. Ƞ is 74.56% at 0.5 A/g and reaches 98.28% at 5 A/g, indicates the stability of the electrode materials. As the long cycling life is a desired metric of any efficient supercapacitor device, the cycling stability test is performed by repeating the charge–discharge measurements of the assembled device at a constant current density of 3 A /g in the potential window 0–1.4 V over 5000 cycles as shown in Fig. 6c. Upon cycling, the specific capacitance of the device increased gradually due to the full activation of the electrode surface as the surface functional groups are expected to increase the wettability and facilitate the continuous diffusion of the electrolyte ions into the electrode. We noticed some capacitance fluctuations, which can be related to temperature changes upon continues charging and discharging [45]. The shape of the charge–discharge curves remains almost unchanged, indicating a high electrochemical stability, revealing the excellent performance of the electrode material composed of a conductive matrix (carbon dots) and the amorphous structure of electrode material that suppressed the volume change during the charging and discharging processes. The Coulombic efficiency of the asymmetric supercapacitor retained nearly 98% during the charge–discharge cycling process [46-48], indicating the great utility of the assembled device.

Energy density and power density are the most important metrics that determine the performance of supercapacitor devices in real applications. Ragone plot shows the relationship between the power density (P, W/kg) and the energy density (E, Wh/kg) of the assembled device at different current densities as shown in Fig. 5d. The E&P can be calculated from the GCD curves using Eqs. (8) and (9):
(8)
E =
1 2
C
s p
Δ
V 2
(9)
P
=
E
t d
where ΔV is the potential window and td is the discharge time obtained from the GCD graph. The assembled asymmetric supercapacitor delivers power density of 350 W/kg and energy density of 20.01 Wh/kg at a current density 0.5 A/g. Upon increasing the current density of the device to 5 A/g, the power density increases to reach 3500 W/kg with a corresponding energy density of 8.8 Wh/kg. Table 1 compares our results to those reported in literature. To get more insights into the electrochemical performance of the asymmetric supercapacitor, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out at open circuit voltage over a wide frequency range (100 kHz–0.01 Hz) to study the capacitive behavior of the MoS2/NCDs electrodes and to elucidate the internal resistance. Fig. 6e shows the EIS of the device before and after cycling stability with the Nyquist plot fitted to an equivalent circuit composed of electrolyte resistance (R1=2.5182 Ω) and charge transfer resistance (R2=0.2716 Ω). At high frequency, there is no or very small semicircle, which reflects the conductivity of the electrode materials and the electrolyte. The tilted line in the high-to-medium-frequency region may be related to the diffusion of K + ions beneath the MoS2 layers and charge transfer resistance, which is related to the Warburg impedance. At low frequency, the semi-straight line refers to the capacitive behavior. These results indicate that the addition of CDs possessed a positive effect on decreasing the electrochemical impedance of MoS2 [49,50].

Flower-like MoS2/NCDs composite electrodes with dense stretching petal-like edges were successfully synthesized via a facile solvothermal method. The as-synthesized composite manifests excellent electrochemical properties in 6.0 M KOH. The CV results showed that this material exhibited a pseudocapacitive behavior, where the alkali metal cation (K+) diffuses and intercalates between the MoS2 layers. Further, an asymmetric supercapacitor device based on AC and MoS2/NCDs as the negative and positive electrodes, respectively was fabricated, which delivers an energy density of 20.01 Wh/kg, a power density of 350 W/kg, and a Coulombic efficiency of 98% after 5000 cycles, indicating excellent cycling stability. The superior electrochemical performance of the MoS2/NCDs composite is ascribed to its superior electronic conductivity and large specific surface area.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of the work by ASRT is highly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.est.2019.101078.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) derived nanoporous carbon: the effect of carbonization temperature on the supercapacitor performance in an aqueous electrolyte

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Young, R. R. Salunkhe, J. Tang, C.-C. Hu, Mo. Shahabuddin, E. Yanmaz, M.S.A. Hossain, J.H. Kimbf and Y. Yamauchi, Zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) derived nanoporous carbon: the effect of carbonization temperature on the supercapacitor performance in an aqueous electrolyte, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys., 18 (2016) 29308–29315.
